paper_title: DeonticBench: A Benchmark for Reasoning over Rules

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Reasoning with complex, context-specific rules remains challenging for large language models (LLMs). In legal and policy settings, this manifests as deontic reasoning: reasoning about obligations, permissions, and prohibitions under explicit rules. While many recent benchmarks emphasize short-context mathematical reasoning, fewer focus on long-context, high-stakes deontic reasoning. To address this gap, we introduce DEONTICBENCH, a benchmark of 6,232 tasks across U.S. federal taxes, airline baggage policies, U.S. immigration administration, and U.S. state housing law. These tasks can be approached in multiple ways, including direct reasoning in language or with the aid of symbolic computation. Besides free-form chain-of-thought reasoning, DEONTICBENCH enables an optional solver-based workflow in which models translate statutes and case facts into executable Prolog, leading to formal problem interpretations and an explicit program trace. We release reference Prolog programs for all instances. Across frontier LLMs and coding models, best hard-subset performance reaches only 44.4% on SARA Numeric and 46.6 macro-F1 on Housing. We further study training with supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning for symbolic program generation. Although training improves Prolog generation quality, current RL methods still fail to solve these tasks reliably. Overall, DEONTICBENCH provides a benchmark for studying context-grounded rule reasoning in real-world domains under both symbolic and non-symbolic settings.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: In February 2026, the New York State Senate Committee passed Senate Bill S7263 (New York State Senate, 2026), establishing liability for harms caused by AI chatbots impersonating licensed professionals. This case reflects broader trend: as large language models (LLMs) are deployed in high-stakes settings such as healthcare (He et al., 2025) and legal systems (Dehghani et al., 2025), errors can create legal, financial, and safety risks. Yet even with rapid progress and wider adoption, LLMs remain prone to hallucinations (Huang et al., 2025; Bengio et al., 2026). Ensuring that LLMs behave faithfully, especially when reasoning over formal rules, is therefore critical. Language model reasoning using chain-of-Thought (CoT; Wei et al., 2022; Guo et al., 2025) shows strong gains on challenging tasks, yet exhibits key failure modes, including harderto-detect hallucinations and errors on simple cases (Cheng et al., 2025; Shojaee et al., 2025). In high-stakes settings, these limitations are especially concerning because models must be not only accurate but also faithful to explicit rules, making deontic reasoningreasoning about obligations, permissions, and prohibitions under formal statutes and policiesan important capability to evaluate. In parallel, symbolic reasoning maps natural-language problems into executable code run by symbolic solvers, offering potential path toward more faithful and interpretable rule-grounded reasoning (Pan et al., 2023; Mahdavi et al., 2024; Ye et al., 2023; Lyu et al., 2023). Correspondence to: gdou1@jhu.edu. 1Code and data are available at guangyaodou/DeonticBench. 1 Preprint. Under review. Figure 1: Walkthrough of DEONTICBENCH instance in the symbolic setting. (1) Given the full problem context, the model performs deontic reasoning to identify and apply the relevant rules. (2) The LLM translates the problem into Prolog code. (3) The generated Prolog is executed by SWI-Prolog solver. The illustrated example is 2017 tax-liability case. To study this capability, we introduce DEONTICBENCH, benchmark of 6,232 tasks spanning U.S. federal taxes, airline baggage policies, U.S. immigration administration, and U.S. state housing law. DEONTICBENCH extends the mathematical-reasoning and STEM-benchmark paradigm (Cobbe et al., 2021; Glazer et al., 2024; Hendrycks et al., 2020) to high-stakes, statute-grounded rule reasoning. It includes both quantitative tasks and binary deontic-logic tasks. Building on prior work on statutory reasoning with symbolic solvers (Holzenberger & Van Durme, 2023; Jurayj et al., 2026), we include solver-assisted workflow in which models can map rules and case facts to executable Prolog code (Colmerauer et al., 1973). This workflow provides auditable reasoning artifactsa formal interpretation of the input and an explicit program tracewhile our evaluation also includes non-symbolic settings. We release reference Prolog code for all instances as artifacts for analysis and training, and we evaluate models under both symbolic and non-symbolic settings. Previous work suggests that the advantage of the symbolic setting is task-dependentsome tasks benefit more from symbolic methods, while others do not (Chen et al., 2024). We evaluate eight general-purpose LLMs (including five reasoning models) and three coding models on DEONTICBENCH. We find that all models struggle on the hard subsets of the benchmark ( 2.5), and that increased reasoning effort does not consistently improve performance. We further post-train Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct (Yang et al., 2024a) with supervised fine-tuning and both on-policy and off-policy reinforcement learning, improving performance, but yielding limited gains in end-to-end task accuracy. In summary, our contributions are threefold: (1) we introduce DEONTICBENCH, the largest benchmark to date for executable rule-based reasoning in real-world, high-stakes domains; (2) we provide comprehensive evaluation and analysis of failure modes in current frontier models, finding that all evaluated models struggle on hard subsets; and (3) we investigate training strategies, highlighting the limitations of existing reinforcement learning approaches and the need for further advances.

sections.title: 2 DEONTICBENCH Dataset Construction
sections.content: DEONTICBENCH is multi-domain deontic reasoning benchmark that enables symbolic and non-symbolic settings. One symbolic workflow is illustrated in Figure 1. 2 Preprint. Under review.

sections.title: 2.1 Benchmark Construction
sections.content: DEONTICBENCH covers four real-world rule domains: U.S. federal taxes (SARA) (Holzenberger & Van Durme, 2023), airline baggage policies (Airline) (Zhou et al., 2025), U.S. state housing law (Housing) (Zheng et al., 2025), and U.S. immigration administration (USCISAAO). All tasks follow unified input-output format, but the domains vary in answer type and reasoning demands, including numeric computation, logical entailment, and multi-factor legal decision-making. The benchmark expands prior data rather than simply reusing it. We combine three existing domains (SARA, Airline, and Housing) with newly constructed USCIS-AAO dataset, and convert all domains to unified task format. We also add reference Prolog programs for each instance to encode corresponding rules and case facts. This supports consistent crossdomain evaluation in both symbolic and non-symbolic settings. Representative examples from each domain are provided in Appendix E.

sections.title: 2.2 Curated and Expanded Source Datasets
sections.content: SARA (U.S. Federal Tax). The SARA subset in DEONTICBENCH adapts the original SARA (Holzenberger & Van Durme, 2023) and retains two task types. SARA Numeric asks models to compute federal tax liability from income and filing status. SARA Binary asks whether statute-grounded claim follows from the statute and case facts (e.g., Entailment or Contradiction? Alices total exemption for 2015 under Section 151(a) is equal to $4000.). The original SARA dataset provides Prolog code for each case, but assumes the full tax statute has already been encoded as background rules. In contrast, DEONTICBENCH adopts more realistic setup: for each case, the model requires Prolog generation on the fly from the statute and case context, without relying on pre-encoded background-rule program. Airline (Airline Baggage Policies). The Airline cases are subset from RuleArena (Zhou et al., 2025). The Airline domain focuses on numeric baggage fee computation. Given passengers ticket status, baggage attributes, and the corresponding airline policy (statute), models should determine the total applicable fees by correctly applying structured airline baggage policy rules. Housing (U.S. State Housing). Housing cases in DEONTICBENCH are the Housing Statute QA problems from legal retrieval setting using RAG (Zheng et al., 2025), which poses binary yes/no questions grounded in state-level housing statutes. Specifically, we look at the first split (rc questions) that contains 6,853 question-answer pair examples with labeled supporting statutes. In these cases, models must identify the relevant statutory provisions and apply them to case-specific scenarios across diverse jurisdictions.

sections.title: 2.3 New Dataset: USCIS-AAO
sections.content: USCIS-AAO is binary immigration-appeal dataset in which each case is labeled as accepted or dismissed. Solving each task requires applying multifactor legal criteria from Administrative Appeals Office case records. Data Source and Sampling The dataset was constructed from publicly available Administrative Appeals Office (AAO) non-precedent decisions.2 These records document appeals of prior United States Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) decisions and U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) determinations. We retained only cases with consistent structure suitable for dataset construction and analysis. Specifically, case was considered valid if it contained three distinct, non-overlapping sections: Law (statutory basis), Analysis (case reasoning), and Order (final decision). Cases were collected in USCIS upload order, yielding 6,483 valid cases from 20222025. We restricted the study to this time window to keep analyses within relatively consistent prece2USCIS AAO non-precedent decisions. 3 Preprint. Under review. dent regime: newer decisions can complement, modify, or reinforce existing interpretations (Appendix D.1). We then selected 242 cases using stratified sampling by decision label and year. The source distribution was highly imbalanced (Appendix D), with only 20.1% of valid cases labeled Accepted. This imbalance constrained the size of the final balanced subset. After filtering and balancing, the final dataset contains 242 cases: 121 Accepted and 121 Dismissed. Label Mapping and Fact Extraction Within this framework, accepted decisions refer to cases that are either remanded for further revision or where the benefit petition is directly approved. In this study, remanded cases were classified as accepted. remand indicates that the appeal has been accepted, although final decision regarding the benefit cannot yet be rendered, typically due to missing evidence or procedural errors by the original office (Appendix D.1). More detailed dataset statistics can be found in Table 8. primary challenge in the construction process is that inferring the decision (ground truth) of each case from the analysis section is not valid, as this section contains AAO judgments, assessments, and strong indications of the final decision. To address this issue, facts-only narrative was generated and stored as new field within the artifact. This extraction was conducted using the GPT-5-mini model, followed by human revision. The prompt utilized is provided in appendix A. The released artifact presents each case divided into three raw sections and includes the model-generated facts-only narrative.

sections.title: 2.4 Prolog Generation Pipeline
sections.content: consider two LLM-to-Prolog We pipelines. One pre-translates each unique statute into reusable Prolog library. The other generates statute modules on the fly per instance and compile-checks them in SWI-Prolog solver (Wielemaker et al., 2012). We adopt the second design because failures are localized to each statutequestion pair, and successful generations transfer cleanly to updated corpora without maintaining global library. sections.title: 2.6 Dataset Statistics
sections.content: Table 1 summarizes the size and token statistics of DEONTICBENCH across domains and splits. In SARA and Airline, the statutes (e.g., tax law or carrier policies) are fixed across cases. On the other hand, Housing and USCIS involve case-specific statutes or legal references, leading to shorter but more variable inputs. We can also observe that the hard subsets generally require more complex reasoning, and hence, longer Prolog codes to solve. Concretely, mean Prolog length increases in 4 out of 5 domains: 945 to 1236 (SARA Numeric), 361 to 453 (SARA Binary), 880 to 1034 (Airline), and 884 to 956 (USCIS-AAO).

sections.title: 3 Experimental Setup
sections.content: We evaluate diverse set of frontier and open-source language models on DEONTICBENCH, which requires long-context reasoning over executable rule systems: GPT-4.1, GPT-5.1, 5 Preprint. Under review. GPT-5.2, O3, Claude 4.5 Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Kimi K2 Instruct 0905, and Qwen3235B-A22B-Instruct (Singh et al., 2025; OpenAI, 2025b; Team et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2025). Additional evaluation details are provided in Appendix B.3.

sections.title: 3.1 Prompting Strategies
sections.content: We evaluate three prompting strategies: Direct, Zero-shot, and Few-shot. Direct prompting predicts the final answer without generating Prolog. Zero-shot prompting generates complete executable Prolog program from statutes and case facts under domain-specific output constraints for automatic execution and scoring. Few-shot prompting uses the same program-synthesis format with in-context exemplars. Full prompt templates and formatting constraints are provided in Appendix B.4.

sections.title: 3.2 Metrics
sections.content: For SARA Numeric and Airline, we report accuracy with $1 tolerance on the solver output. For SARA Binary, Housing, and USCIS-AAO, we report macro-F1. We also track two error categories: incorrect predictions and abstentions (non-executable Prolog or unparsable direct outputs) (Jurayj et al., 2026), and treat abstentions as incorrect during scoring. We report results on domain-specific hard subsets with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. For each case, we sample generations to capture output stochasticity (K=4 for SARA Numeric and Binary, and USCIS-AAO; K=3 for Airline and Housing). Full metric definitions, abstention mapping rules, and the bootstrap procedure are provided in Appendix B.3.

sections.title: 4 Results
sections.content: We evaluate language models on DEONTICBENCH hard sets across multiple domains and prompting strategies, focusing on both overall task performance and systematic failure modes. We also analyze model behavior through detailed error taxonomy.

sections.title: 4.1 Main Results
sections.content: Reasoning over rules remains challenging for language models. As Table 2 shows, performance varies by domain and prompting strategy: few-shot helps Airline but not SARA Numeric, while zero-shot and direct solving often work better on binary tasks (SARA Binary, USCIS-AAO) than on Housing. Stronger frontier models (e.g., GPT-5.1, O3, Claude Sonnet 4.5) perform best overall, but none is consistently best across domains or settings. Notably, confidence intervals remain wide across hard subsets, in both smaller hard subsets (e.g., SARA Numeric) and larger ones (e.g., Airline and Housing), suggesting variability beyond sample-size effects. There is clear gap between frontier and open-source models. Open-source models perform worse in few-shot and zero-shot settings and are more prompt-sensitive: Qwen3235B, for instance, rises from near-random few-shot performance (0.7 on SARA Numeric) to 32.1 under direct prompting. The gap narrows on binary tasks, where direct prompting yields competitive scores (66.5 on SARA Binary for Qwen3-235B and 68.4 for Kimi K2), but open-source models remain less reliable for exact rule-based computation. Performance decomposition reveals systematic failure modes. We analyze the proportion of correct predictions, incorrect answers, and abstentions for each model and prompting strategy, as shown in Figure 2. Across all the domains, large fraction of errors arise from incorrect answers rather than abstentions. In particular, many models produce executable but incorrect Prolog programs, or return wrong answers under direct prompting. However, when solving tasks via Prolog, models exhibit higher abstention rates, whereas direct prompting reduces abstentions but increases the frequency of incorrect predictions. This highlights trade-off between Preprint. Under review. (a) SARA Numeric (b) Airline Figure 2: Performance decomposition analysis for SARA Numeric and Airline. Each model shows three bars (left to right: Direct, Zero-Shot, Few-Shot). Plots for other domains are in Figure 4 (Appendix C.3). coverage and reliability. These results motivate confidence-aware inference. With finegrained abstention calibration, smaller model can handle high-confidence cases, while uncertain cases are deferred to stronger model. This improves cost efficiency and reduces risk in high-stakes domains; developing confidence signals remains future work. Additional analyses. Extended results for coding LLMs and reasoning-effort ablations are detailed in the appendix (Appendix C.1; Appendix C.2).

sections.title: 4.2 Performance of Locally Trained Models
sections.content: Table 3 reports performance on the hard subsets of DEONTICBENCH when fine-tuning Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct (Yang et al., 2024a). We ensure that hard-set examples are excluded from training, as described in 2.5. Following Unilaw-R1 (Cai et al., 2025) and OLMO 3 training recipe (Olmo et al., 2025), we apply Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) (Rafailov et al., 2023), and Dr. GRPO (Liu et al., 2025), variant of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) (Shao et al., 2024) with predicate-aware reward function inspired by Chen & Tam (2025). Additional training details are provided in Appendix B.5. Fine-tuning improves classification but not numeric reasoning. SFT improves performance over the base model, especially on binary tasks such as SARA Binary and USCIS-AAO (e.g., few-shot USCIS-AAO rises from 10.3 to over 45 with alignment-based training). By contrast, SARA Numeric remains below 10 across methods and prompting strategies, indicating that precise rule-based computation is still key bottleneck. 7 Preprint. Under review. Reinforcement Learning provides additional gains, but inconsistently. DPO and Dr. GRPO generally outperform SFT alone, especially in few-shot settings. For instance, GRPO+DPO+SFT achieves the strongest performance on Airline and SARA Binary. However, these gains are not uniform across domains or prompting strategies, and improvements are limited on more challenging reasoning tasks. Prompt sensitivity persists after training. After post-hoc training, models remain highly sensitive to prompting strategy. Few-shot prompting consistently yields the best performance on Airline, while direct prompting is more effective for binary reasoning tasks. Overall, while SFT and RL improve performance, current RLHF-based methods are insufficient for robust rule-grounded reasoning on DEONTICBENCH. Developing methods that reliably produce correct, executable Prolog remains an important direction for future work.

sections.title: 4.3 Failure Modes in Prolog Reasoning
sections.content: By comparing model-generated Prolog and ground truth Prolog, we categorize errors on the hard cases made by frontier models (GPT-5.1, GPT-5.2, O3) into four types: Wrong Rule, Entity / Fact, Numerical, and Implementation Bugs. Table 4 reports the distribution of these error types across domains and prompting strategies. We can observe that rule selection errors dominate in legally complex settings such as Housing and USCIS-AAO, where models frequently apply incorrect statutes or misinterpret eligibility criteria. This suggests that retrieving and grounding the correct legal rule remains primary bottleneck, even when models can generate syntactically valid Prolog. Second, entity and fact extraction errors are particularly prominent in SARA tasks, especially under zero-shot prompting. In these cases, models often identify the correct rule but incorrectly encode case-specific details (e.g., filing status or dependency relationships), leading to incorrect outcomes despite otherwise valid reasoning structure. Overall, these results highlight that failures are domain-dependent: legal domains are bottlenecked by rule selection, structured reasoning tasks by fact extraction, and quantitative 8 Preprint. sections.title: 5.1 Related Datasets for Deontic Reasoning
sections.content: Rule-reasoning benchmarks have expanded across formal and natural-language settings: FOLIO (Han et al., 2024), LogicBench (Parmar et al., 2024), and JustLogic (Chen et al., 2025) emphasize formal structure and representation alignment, while ProofWriter (Tafjord et al., 2021) and ProntoQA (Saparov & He, 2022) focus on multi-step entailment and synthetic first-order-logic reasoning with verifiable proof chains. Recent process-oriented benchmarks include LOGicalThought (Nananukul et al., 2025), which targets negation, implication, and defeasible reasoning, and ContextBench (Li et al., 2026), which evaluates intermediate context retrieval for coding agents using human-annotated gold contexts. RuleArena (Zhou et al., 2025) and CL-bench (Dou et al., 2026) are the closest to our setting. Compared with RuleArena and CL-bench, DEONTICBENCH is larger (6,232 vs. 816 tasks in RuleArena), covers additional high-stakes legal domains, and provides executable Prolog artifacts for program-trace analysis. CL-bench refers to this setup as context learning; related formulations also appear in prior statutory-reasoning work (Holzenberger & Van Durme, 2023; Blair-Stanek et al., 2023). Like CL-bench, DEONTICBENCH minimizes reliance on parametric knowledge by providing all required statutes and facts in each instance.

sections.title: 5.2 Symbolic Reasoning in Large Language Models
sections.content: Recent work on LLM reasoning integrates structured symbolic methods, often referred to as autoformalization (Wu et al., 2022; Mensfelt et al., 2026). common neuro-symbolic line combines neural modules with probabilistic logic for differentiable or approximate inference (van Krieken et al., 2023; Pryor et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2021). Another common approach uses two-stage pipeline: translate natural language into symbolic programs, then execute them with external solvers. Faithful Chain-of-Thought (Lyu et al., 2023) and Logic-LM (Pan et al., 2023) show that executable formalisms such as PDDL and Prolog can improve faithfulness. LOGicalThought (Nananukul et al., 2025) similarly maps natural language into symbolic representations and reasons with an external solver (ErgoAI). 9 Preprint. Under review. More recent work studies direct Prolog generation. For example, prior studies show that LLMs can generate Prolog programs for arithmetic reasoning (Yang et al., 2024b). In legal reasoning, Jurayj et al. (2026) show that combining LLMs with Prolog solvers improves statutory reasoning. Our work builds on these directions and provides Prolog reference for all the instances.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduce DEONTICBENCH, benchmark of 6,232 high-stakes, long-context rulereasoning tasks spanning U.S. federal taxes, airline baggage policies, U.S. immigration administration, and U.S. state housing law. DEONTICBENCH supports both direct language reasoning and solver-assisted execution with reference Prolog programs. Across frontier reasoning and coding models, performance remains weak on difficult subsets, and added reasoning effort does not consistently help. Although SFT and RL improve performance, reliable end-to-end gains remain limited.

sections.title: Ethics Statement
sections.content: This benchmark studies model behavior in high-stakes legal and policy domains, where errors can cause harm if systems are deployed without appropriate human oversight. We release DEONTICBENCH strictly for research and evaluation purposes, not for operational decision-making. Model outputs should not be treated as professional legal, tax, or policy advice.

sections.title: A Additional Prompts
sections.content: Additional Instructions for Facts Extraction. The following instruction is used to prompt GPT-5-mini to extract factual information from the content of USCIS cases: USCIS facts extraction prompt Use this administrative appeals office appeal case to extract only the facts related (cid:44) (cid:44) (cid:44) (cid:44) (cid:44) (cid:44) (cid:44) (cid:44) (cid:44) (cid:44) to the petitioner (not the analysis of the administrative appeals office director) and the reason of appealing. Avoid including any application of the law. Make it description of only the facts and the reason of appealing. It should not contain any analysis of the described rules or other rules or precedents that are not strictly facts stated by the petitioner. This description of facts should be written in natural language and coherent when reading. It shouldn't be only list of facts but description of the facts as if it where human description of them. Avoid stating the conclusion of the case or what was resolved. The opinion and resolution of the administrative appeals office must not be included. Case text under analysis: {case_text}

sections.title: B Additional Experiment Setup
sections.content: B.1 Prolog Generation Pipeline Details We consider two LLM-to-Prolog generation pipelines. In the first, we pre-translate each unique statute into Prolog module, compile-check it under SWI-Prolog (Wielemaker et al., 2012), and store it as reusable library. For each question, the model then generates only the question program that calls precompiled statute predicates. In the second, we use OpenAI o3 (OpenAI, 2025b) (medium reasoning effort) to generate statute modules on the fly, compile-check them, and then generate the question program against those interfaces. This avoids maintaining global library and better supports frequently changing, jurisdiction-dependent regulations. In our experiments, we use the second pipeline. This design localizes failures to single statute/question artifact pair and allows successful generations to transfer cleanly to updated corpora without maintaining monolithic global library. Each generated artifact (statute modules + question program) is executed and counted as passing only if (1) it compiles and runs cleanly in SWI-Prolog and (2) the emitted answer matches the gold label. Failures include incorrect answers, missing or extra output, missing code, module import errors, Prolog warnings, and execution timeouts (20s). During curation, we retain only <Statutes + Case Facts, Prolog> pairs with passing artifacts. We first generate an initial batch of Prolog programs for small subset in each domain, then send random subset to human experts for validation. Curation proceeds with checklist: annotators verify (1) rule coverage (all legally relevant conditions are encoded), (2) fact fidelity (case facts are preserved without label leakage), (3) executability of Prolog programs (no warnings/import/runtime issues), and (4) answer agreement with the gold label; artifacts that fail are regenerated, and each edited artifact is re-executed before acceptance. After curation, we stratify accepted examples by domain, sample small balanced set as few-shot demonstrations (mixing positive/negative outcomes and common edge cases), and regenerate the remaining instances using this curated few-shot examples. To increase the number of passing artifacts, we allow up to two attempts per instance: on failure, we re-prompt with the previous code and SWI-Prolog compiler/runtime output; if the second attempt fails, the instance is discarded. This mirrors tool-integrated evaluation settings where generated programs are executed and verified by external systems, yielding deterministic feedback signals rather than purely textual judgments (Yang et al., 2023). For USCIS-AAO, we apply additional syntax-focused prompt constraints to reduce parsing and compilation errors, and we encourage static final proof clause when possible. Full prompts and query templates are provided in Appendix and Appendix B.4. 15 Preprint. Under review. B.2 Detailed Hard Set Construction We identify candidate hard instances by running three frontier modelsOpenAI o3, GPT5.2, and Claude 4.5 Sonneton every DEONTICBENCH problem with Prolog generation, using two runs per model. Any instance for which at least one generation fails is marked as potentially hard. We then conduct iterative human review to remove noisy or ambiguous cases and retain only genuinely challenging instances. After this process, we do not place all validated hard instances into the final hard subset. Instead, we partition these validated hard problems into two portions: one is kept as the final hard set for evaluation, and the remainder is returned to the full dataset for training and related experiments. This strategy keeps evaluation focused while ensuring that training still includes challenging examples rather than being dominated by easier cases. B.3 Detailed Metrics and Bootstrap Procedure Accuracy. For SARA Numeric and Airline, prediction is correct if the value obtained from SWI-Prolog is within $1 of the ground truth. Formally, given model output and ground truth ˆy, correctness is defined as round(y) int( ˆy) 1. Macro-F1. For SARA Binary, Housing, and USCIS-AAO, we report macro-F1. Given confusion matrix counts TP, FP, FN, and TN, the class-wise scores are 2TP+FP+FN and (1) 1 = 2TP (0) 1 = 2TN 2TN+FN+FP , and macro-F1 is (0) (1) 1 +F 1 2 . Abstentions and scoring. We distinguish two failure types: (i) incorrect predictions from usable outputs, and (ii) abstentions, where the model output cannot be used for scoring (e.g., non-executable Prolog or unparsable direct output). Abstentions are counted as incorrect predictions. For SARA Numeric and Airline, abstentions are assigned $-1. For SARA Binary, Housing, and USCIS-AAO, abstentions are mapped to the opposite class before computing the confusion matrix. Bootstrap confidence intervals. All main-table metrics are computed on hard subsets. To estimate uncertainty, we use bootstrap resampling with = 1000 replicates. In each replicate, we sample cases with replacement (where is the hard-subset size), then sample one of the outputs per case uniformly at random to account for output stochasticity. We report 95% confidence intervals using the empirical 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles. We use = 4 for SARA Numeric, SARA Binary, and USCIS-AAO, and = 3 for Airline and Housing. B.4 Zero-Shot Prompt Templates This appendix presents the full prompt templates used in the Zero-Shot (Standalone Prolog) condition for each domain. Placeholders like this are replaced at inference time with the corresponding case-specific content. All prompts are preceded by the system message: You are helpful assistant trained to conduct deontic reasoning.

sections.title: SARA Numeric
sections.content: SARA Numeric Zero-Shot Prompt Template ## Statutes: statutes ## Case: case text ## Question: question The question above asks about the case in relation to the statutes. First, write 16 Preprint. Under review. logic program in prolog to encode the relevant rules defined in the statutes. Then write logic program in prolog to encode the facts and rules contained in the case above. Then write query to compute and print the value the question asks. You must indicate your prolog code using: ```prolog <YOUR LOGIC PROGRAM HERE> ``` For instance, to answer How much tax does Samuel owe in 1992, your final lines should be ```prolog :- tax("Samuel", 1992, Tax), format(Tax result: wn, [Tax]). :- halt. ```

sections.title: SARA Binary
sections.content: SARA Binary Zero-Shot Prompt Template ## Statutes: statutes ## Case: case text ## Claim: claim The claim above makes statement about the case in relation to the statutes. Determine whether the claim is correct (Entailment) or incorrect (Contradiction). First, write logic program in Prolog to encode the relevant rules defined in the statutes. Then write logic program in Prolog to encode the facts contained in the case above. Then write query that checks whether the claim holds, and prints the result. You must indicate your prolog code using: ```prolog <YOUR LOGIC PROGRAM HERE> ``` Your programs final output must be exactly one of: Result: Entailment Result: Contradiction For example, your final lines should follow this pattern: ```prolog :- ( <your verification goal> -> format(Result: Entailmentn) ; format(Result: Contradictionn) ). :- halt. ``` Follow this format exactly. Think step-by-step before answering.

sections.title: Airline
sections.content: Airline Zero-Shot Prompt Template ## Statutes: statutes ## Case: case text 17 Preprint. Under review. ## Question: question Write complete runnable SWI-Prolog program that computes the answer. Indicate your prolog code using: ```prolog <YOUR LOGIC PROGRAM HERE> ``` Final lines should print total result and halt.

sections.title: Housing
sections.content: Legal IR Zero-Shot Prompt Template You are an expert US housing lawyer and senior SWI-Prolog engineer. self-contained SWI-Prolog program that translates statutes and question facts. Write ### HousingQA Sample - state: state - focus year: 2021 ### Question question ### Natural Language Statutes statutes ### Output requirements - Output one runnable ```prolog block only. - Define housing answer(Result) where Result is yes or no. - Derive Result via statute predicates; do not hardcode. - The file must run correctly under swipl -f file.pl. - Define main/0 and end with exactly :- initialization(main, main). - main must print exactly: housing answer(w). USCIS-AAO USCIS-AAO Zero-Shot Prompt Template You are an expert in the analysis of immigration appeals and you will parse these Administrative Appeals Office cases into Prolog code. Parse the facts and rules into Prolog code, ensuring that the programs output is binary: either Dismissed or Accepted. You are given the following facts: case facts You are given the following rules: statutory rules For true/false or yes/no predicates, use arity 0 and check it is consistent for all clauses. For example, use there is evidence. instead of there is evidence(True). Include this structure at the end and work assuming that eligibility met is all the conditions necessary to Accept the case. Therefore, this must be the last part of the program. Do not include the query in the prolog output, only include the entrypoint. When executed, print exactly one token: Accepted or Dismissed. Include this structure at the end (where eligibility met encodes all conditions necessary to accept the case): decision(Result) :- 18 Preprint. Under review. ( eligibility met -> Result = Accepted ; Result = Dismissed ). main :- catch( ( decision(Result), writeln(Result) ), error(existence error(procedure, PI), ), handle undefined(PI) ). handle undefined(Name/Arity) :- ( current predicate(Name/OtherArity), OtherArity = Arity -> format(Programming error: called w/w, but only w/w is defined.n, [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity]) ; format(Lack of information: predicate w/w is not defined.n, [Name, Arity]) ).

B.5 Additional Details of Locally Trained Models In this section we go through more in-depth details of the training pipeline mentioned in section 4.2. Specifically, we train Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct through Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and Reinforcement Learning. B.5.1 SFT and DPO Data Preparation In SFT, we formatted the data in Alpaca Format with Statute + Contexts, Prolog pairs. For DPO, we need to generate preference dataset, which includes chosen (the ground truth Prolog in DEONTICBENCH) and, following the delta learning hypothesis (Geng et al., 2025), we generate rejected answers by using the off-the-shelf base model. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) In SFT, the model is trained to maximize the likelihood of the ground-truth Prolog program given the input (statutes and case context). The 19 Preprint. Under review. objective is the standard negative log-likelihood: LSFT(θ) = (x,y)D (cid:35) log pθ(yt x, y<t) , (cid:34) t= (1) where yt denotes the t-th token in the target sequence. Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) DPO optimizes the model using preference triples (x, y+, y), where y+ is the preferred output and is the dispreferred output. Following Rafailov et al. (2023), the objective is LDPO(θ) = (x,y+,y)D (cid:20) (cid:18) log σ β log πθ(y+ x) πref(y+ x) β log (cid:19)(cid:21) πθ(y x) πref(y x) , (2) where σ() is the sigmoid function, β controls the strength of the preference optimization, and πref is the reference policy. SFT and DPO Training Configuration We use LLaMA Factory (Zheng et al., 2024) to run SFT and DPO with configuration specified in Table 5. We train these models on 4 H100 GPUs. B.5.2 Reinforcement Learning DR. GRPO For reinforcement learning, we adopt Dr. GRPO (Liu et al., 2025), an unbiased variant of GRPO. (3) (4) (5) Reward Function The scalar reward R(q, oi) is computed by executing the generated Prolog program with SWI-Prolog (20-second timeout) and comparing its output against ground-truth label. We define three-level scheme: (1) Correct execution. The response is parsed for fenced prolog code block, which is then run through SWI-Prolog. If the printed output matches the ground-truth label exactly, the model receives the full reward: R(q, oi) = 1. (6) If the extracted code is non-executable (syntax or (2) Predicate-overlap partial credit. runtime error), we compare the set of predicate signatures (name/arity pairs, after stripping comments) between the generated program and the reference program P. The partial reward is proportional to their Jaccard similarity: R(q, oi) = S(P) S(P) S(P) S(P) δ, δ = 0.2, (7) where S() denotes the set of predicate signatures extracted from program. This gives small signal (R [0, 0.2]) that rewards structural alignment with the reference even when the code fails to run. 20 Preprint. Under review. (3) All other cases. reward of 0 is assigned when no Prolog block is found, the code times out, or the code runs but produces the wrong answer. The binary nature of the correctness signal (1 vs. 0 for executable outputs) encourages the model to produce Prologs that compute the exact right answer, while the predicateoverlap term provides weak gradient for non-executable generations to adopt the correct predicates. Dr. GRPO Training Config Table 6 contains the training details of Dr. GRPO. We use VeRL (Sheng et al., 2024) with 4 H200 GPUs.

sections.title: C Additional Experiment Results
sections.content: C.1 Performance of Coding LLMs We also evaluate the performance of GPT-5.2-Codex (OpenAI, 2025a), Qwen3-CoderNext-FP8, and Qwen/Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B-Instruct-FP8 (Cao et al., 2026) on DEONTICBENCH. The results are shown in Table 7. GPT-5.2-Codex achieves the strongest overall performance, yet its performance remains sensitive to prompts. In contrast, Qwen-based coding models frequently fail the tasks, resulting in near-zero scores in several settings. This suggests that code generation for rule-grounded reasoning is challenging for coding agents, with small prompt variations leading to catastrophic failures. Additionally, large bootstrap confidence intervals indicate substantial variance across runs, highlighting the instability of Prolog generations. Overall, these results demonstrate that, despite their strong coding capabilities, current coding agents struggle to reliably generate executable Prolog codes for complex, long-context rule reasoning tasks. C.

sections.title: Impact of Reasoning Effort on Performance
sections.content: We further investigate how increasing reasoning effort affects model performance on SARA Numeric. As shown in Figure 3, performance does not consistently improve with higher reasoning effort; in some cases, additional effort leads to diminishing or even negative returns. Moreover, GPT-5.1 consistently outperforms GPT-5.2 across SARA Numeric, SARA Binary, USCIS-AAO, and Housing, and remains competitive on Airline  (Table 2)  . This suggests that stronger general reasoning and instruction-following capabilities do not directly translate to improved performance on DEONTICBENCH. Instead, success in our setting appears to depend more critically on the ability to generate precise, executable reasoning structures. 21 Preprint. Under review. C.3 Additional Failure Mode Analysis In this section, we present additional error bar charts for SARA Binary, Housing, and USCIS-AAO in Figure 4. USCIS-AAO Dataset Descriptive Statistics Table 8 presents the statistics of the USCIS-AAO dataset. D.1 USCIS-AAO Dataset Scope and Preprocessing The AAO possesses defined jurisdiction, function, and policy. This appendix outlines the scope of publicly available materials and details the preprocessing steps used to construct the dataset. AAO Jurisdiction The Administrative Appeals Office (AAO) conducts independent de novo appellate review of United States Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) officers decisions, applying USCIS policies and legal interpretations to each case (U.S. 22 Preprint. Under review. (a) Direct (b) Zero-Shot (c) Few-Shot Figure 3: Reasoning effort ablation on SARA Numeric hard cases. Each panel corresponds to different prompting strategy (Direct, Zero-Shot, and Few-Shot). Bars indicate mean accuracy, with error bars showing 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. Citizenship and Immigration Services, 2020). The AAO functions independently from field offices and reviews all issues of fact, law, policy, and discretion. Upon appeal, the AAO re-examines the record anew, and its decision may address issues that were not previously raised or resolved. The AAO adjudicates three primary categories of cases: appeals, motions, and certifications. Its jurisdiction is determined by both subject matter and form number, and it includes authority over motions to reopen and motions to reconsider its own decisions. Additionally, the AAO may review an initial decision at the request of USCIS officers when case involves an unusually complex or novel issue of law or fact. 23 Preprint. Under review. (a) SARA Binary (b) Housing (c) USCIS-AAO Figure 4: Performance decomposition analysis for SARA Binary, Housing, and USCIS. Each model shows three bars (left to right: Direct, Zero-Shot, Few-Shot). The colored portion represents correct predictions; the solid pink portion represents wrong answers; the hatched pink portion represents abstentions (Prolog timeout or crash). Dataset Temporal Scope AAO non-precedent decisions serve as effective benchmarks because they generally reflect the application of existing law and agency policy to specific factual scenarios, rather than establishing new binding doctrine. However, the governing legal framework evolves over extended periods, as binding standards may shift due to subsequent precedent decisions, adopted AAO decisions, or revisions to USCIS policy guidance. Consequently, constructing benchmark from cases spanning multiple years would conflate two sources of variation: differences in adjudicative reasoning under stable legal regime and changes resulting from modifications to the legal regime itself. Therefore, the dataset is not constructed by pooling cases from widely separated years. The dataset is assembled from restricted subset of years (20222025) to ensure that the applicable legal and policy regime remains approximately stable. This approach reduces 24 Preprint. Under review. the risk of contradictions that arise not from inconsistent reasoning over similar facts, but from changes in governing rules or official guidance over time.

sections.title: Let a decision at time t be written as
sections.content: Dt = Et(F, Rt), where denotes the factual record, Rt denotes the set of governing rules and policy in force at time t, and Et is the adjudicative mapping from facts and rules to an outcome. Over extended time periods, neither Rt nor Et can be assumed constant. The underlying assumption is that variation in outcomes across years is partially attributable to divergence in the governing rule sets. Restricting the benchmark to narrower temporal window serves as an identification strategy, isolating the models ability to model the evaluation function Et under relatively stable regime, rather than rewarding or penalizing the model for shifts in precedent, policy, or official guidance. Facts Extraction Process The Analysis section of each AAO case presents the offices reasoning by applying relevant legal authorities to the evidence in the record to justify the final disposition. This section typically includes evaluations of individual facts, intermediate legal conclusions, and predicate eligibility determinations. The primary objective of the dataset is to assess whether model can predict the case disposition based on the facts and governing legal authorities. Accordingly, the Law section is preserved as near-verbatim statement of the legal authorities governing the case, including relevant statutes, regulations, precedents, and policy materials. In contrast, the Facts value is extracted from the Analysis, where factual statements are often interwoven with the AAOs application of law to facts. This separation was achieved through language-model-assisted extraction followed by human verification. The annotator received only the factual narrative and was instructed to label and exclude any case containing text that revealed the offices application of legal authorities to the facts, an intermediate eligibility determination, or the ultimate outcome. In accordance with this protocol, the released dataset underwent secondary review, which identified additional errors, including text truncation and further contamination. The facts narrative was subsequently re-generated and partially rewritten to ensure that it does not explicitly disclose the cases final resolution. This procedure has important limitations. First, the boundary between factual narrative and legal analysis is partly determined by the annotation protocol and is not entirely objective. Second, the quality of the final artifact depends on the accuracy of the extraction model and the consistency of the human review process. Implementing stricter annotation rubric, involving multiple annotators, and adjudicating disagreements would further enhance the reliability of this filtering procedure.

sections.title: E Dataset Examples
sections.content: We present one representative instance from each domain in DEONTICBENCH. For each domain, we show the relevant statutes (abbreviated where lengthy), the case description, the question, the ground-truth label, and the reference Prolog program (abbreviated where lengthy; omissions marked with % . . . ). E.1 SARA Numeric: U.S. Tax Reasoning SARA Numeric Example Instance Statutes (excerpt) The SARA domain encodes subset of the U.S. Internal Revenue Code, including 1 (tax rates), 2 (filing status), 63 (taxable income), 68 (itemized 25 Preprint. Under review. deduction limitation), 151--152 (exemptions), 3301/3306 (FUTA), and 7703 (marital status). Representative fragment 1(a). Married individuals filing joint returns and surviving spouses. tax is imposed on the taxable income of every married individual who makes single return jointly with his spouse, and every surviving spouse, as follows: (i) 15% if not over $36,900; (ii) $5,535 plus 28% of excess over $36,900 if over $36,900 but not over $89,150; . . . (v) $75,528.50 plus 39.6% of excess over $250,000. Case Alice and Harold got married on Sep 3rd, 1992. Harold and Alice have son, born Jan 25th, 2000. Harold died on Feb 28th, 2016. They had been living in the same house since 1993, maintained by Alice. Alice and her son continued doing so after Harolds death. Alices gross income for the year 2017 was $236,422. Alice employed Bob, Cameron, Dan, Emily, Fred, and George for agricultural labor from Sep 9th to Oct 1st 2017, paying them $5,012 each. Alice takes the standard deduction in 2017. Question How much tax does Alice have to pay in 2017? E.2 SARA Binary: U.S. Tax Entailment Checking SARA Binary Example Instance Note on format Unlike SARA Numeric, SARA Binary is an entailment task: given case description and statement about the applicable law, the model must determine whether the statement is entailed or contradicted by the case under the relevant statutory rules. Statutes (excerpt) The SARA Binary domain draws on the same U.S. Internal Revenue Code provisions as SARA Numeric, focusing on Sections 151--152 (exemptions and dependents). Relevant fragment Sec. 152(c)(1). Qualifying child. An individual is qualifying child of taxpayer for taxable year if the individual bears specified relationship to the taxpayer (Sec. 152(c)(2)), has the same principal place of abode as the taxpayer for more than one-half of the year (Sec. 152(c)(3)), meets the age requirement, has not provided more than one-half of his own support, and has not filed joint return. Sec. 152(b)(1). qualifying child of taxpayer under Sec. 152(c)(1) is treated as dependent of that taxpayer; the provision applies to an individual for any taxable year beginning in the calendar year in which they are qualifying child of any taxpayer. Case Alice has son, Bob, who satisfies section 152(c)(1) for the year 2015. Bob has son, Charlie, who satisfies section 152(c)(1) for the year 2015. Alices income in 2015 was $504,598. Bob had no income in 2015. Question (Statement to verify) Section 152(b)(1) applies to Bob for the year 2015.

E.4 USCIS-AAO: Immigration Appeals USCIS-AAO Example Instance Statutes (excerpt) delivery bond creates contract between the U.S. Government and an obligor. breach occurs upon substantial violation of bonds conditions (8 C.F.R. 103.6(e)); conversely, substantial performance releases the obligor from liability (8 C.F.R. 103.6(c)(3)). Several factors inform whether violation is substantial: the extent of the violation; whether it was intentional or accidental; whether it was in good faith; and whether the obligor took steps to comply. Matter of Kubacki, 18 I&N Dec. 43, 44 (Regl Commr 1981). Case The Obligor posted an ICE Form I-352 immigration delivery bond as security for bonded foreign national. ICE declared the bond breached after the bonded foreign national was not produced in response to written request; the Obligor appealed seeking reinstatement. On appeal, the Obligor argued it could not produce the foreign national because the Notice to Appear (Form I-862) lacked time and place for the hearing, and that under Pereira v. Sessions the defective NTA rendered the proceedings invalid, thereby negating any substantial violation of the bonds terms.
